coredata.dc:title: CeO2/MOH nanosheets with carbon dots for improved photocatalytic N2 fixation performances
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Huaiwei
Wu, Shiting
Bao, Liang
Chen, Yifan
Ge, Jing-yuan
coredata.dc:description: Herein, all-solid-state Z-scheme heterojunction of CeO2-carbon dots-mixed metal hydroxides (MOH) is assembled firstly, in which carbon dots can act as the charge mediators. The material exhibit much higher photocatalytic activity, and the NH3 production rate can reach 528 and 256 µmol·gcal −1·h−1 without any sacrificial agents under the full spectrum and visible-light illumination, respectively. The possible reaction mechanism during the photocatalytic N2 fixation process is also proposed.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Non-noble metal
Heterojunction structures
Solar energy materials
Nanocomposites

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD patterns (a), Raman spectra (b) and HRTEM image of the CeO2-MOH/CDs (c).
XPS spectra of the various samples (a) Ce 3d; (b) Co 2p; (c) Zn 2p; (d) O 1s.
(a) NH3 production rates; (b) NH3 production amounts catalyzed by 1 g of the CeO2-MOH/CDs.

(a) The diffuse reflectance UV–vis spectra (the inset figure reveals the Transformed Kubelka-Munk function); (b) M-S curves; (c) PL emission spectra and (d) Possible mechanism during the N2 fixation process.

head.ce:title: CeO2/MOH nanosheets with carbon dots for improved photocatalytic N2 fixation performances

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Materials Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Materials Engineering, Wenzhou University, Wenzhou 325035, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, all-solid-state Z-scheme heterojunction of CeO2-carbon dots-mixed metal hydroxides (MOH) is assembled firstly, in which carbon dots can act as the charge mediators. The material exhibit much higher photocatalytic activity, and the NH3 production rate can reach 528 and 256 µmol·gcal −1·h−1 without any sacrificial agents under the full spectrum and visible-light illumination, respectively. The possible reaction mechanism during the photocatalytic N2 fixation process is also proposed.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Traditionally, Haber-Bosch process as the main industrialsyntheticmethod could consume more than 1% of world energy Photo-catalysis process is one of the promising technologies for sustainable ammonia synthesis, and introduction the active sites in the photo-catalysts to decrease the higher bond energy (>940 KJ·mol−1) to promote the NN cleavage is therefore necessary [1–3].

Cerium dioxide, with the excellent redox ability, has attracted wide attentions in photocatalytic filed [4–6]. But weaker visible-light absorption and faster electron-hole recombination abilities have restricted its further application. Assembling the CeO2-based heterojunction structures can effectively resolve this problem. Z-scheme heterojunction of CeCO3OH/g-C3N4/CeO2 [7], CeO2-BiFeO3 [8], CeO2 / Ti (Zn, Mn, Fe, Cu)O x
[9–12] and CeO2/LDH [13] catalysts all exhibited the enhanced photo-catalytic activity. The Z-scheme heterojunction usually utilized noble-metal nanomaterials as the electron mediator, which restricted their large-scale applications [14–16]. Hence, the carbon dots are becoming an alternative to assemble the new Z-scheme heterojunctions to improve the visible-light photocatalytic properties [17–19].

In the present research, a facial fabrication strategy of CeO2/CDs/MOH heterojunction photocatalysts with the CDs electrons mediator is reported. The composite exhibits an outstanding photocatalytic performance in the photocatalytic N2 fixation. The CDs doped Z-scheme heterojunction can provide the adding local active sites, which can improve surface defects states and electron-hole separation efficiencies.

Firstly, 0.95 g Zn(NO3)2, 1.46 g Co(NO3)2·6H2O and 1.63 g Ce(NO3)3·6H2O are dissolved in 10 ml of distilled water, and 1.2 g NaOH are also dissolved in 10 ml of distilled water. Then the two solutions are mixed gradually under magnetic stirring and maintained for 1 h at 60 °C. The precipitates are dried at 333 K for 24 h to obtain the CeO2-MOH precursor sample. Subsequently, 0.4 g CeO2- MOH and 0.68 g L-ascorbic acid are mixed in 90 ml distilled water, and ultrasonicdispersed 1 h. The emulsion is transferred into a 120 ml Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 433 K for 24 h. Finally, the precipitates are dried at 333 K for 24 h to obtain CeO2-MOH/CDs. The carbon content was detected in an elemental analyzer, and the C contents in the catalyst was 3.48 wt%. The SEM-EDS analyses of the other elements are shown in the Fig. S1. Nitrogen photo-fixation experiments are operated at room temperature, and the ammonia concentrations are detected through Nessler's reagent [20] . The phase compositions are obtained through XRD and Raman analyses. The surface electron structures are confirmed via an X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy. The microstructures are investigated using the transmission electron microscope. The M-S curves are obtained on an electrochemical station using as-prepared working electrodes. Photoluminescence spectra (PL) are measured on the FLs1000/FS5 fluorescent spectrometer.

The crystal structures are determined through the XRD measurements in Fig. 1 a. The two types of diffraction peaks including the cubic CeO2 (JCPDS #34-0394) and the monoclinic Zn2Co3(OH)10·2H2O (JCPDS #21-1477) are illustrated in the CeO2-MOH and CeO2-MOH/CDs samples, which reveals the successful incorporation between CeO2 and MOH. The Raman spectra of the catalysts are shown in Fig. 1b. A single and strong peak assigned at around 460 cm−1 and 700 cm−1 are assigned to the Ce–O F2g stretching mode and the Zn–Co–O compounds, respectively [21,22]. Two broader peaks of about 1580 and 1320 cm−1 are assigned to the G and D bands of carbon materials in the CeO2-MOH/CDs sample [23]. The HRTEM image of the CeO2-MOH/CDs catalyst is displayed in Fig. 1c. The picture demonstrates the coexistence of three nanostructures of carbon dots (CDs), CeO2 and MOH nanosheets with the lattice distance of 0.21 nm ((100) plane of graphite), 0.75 nm ((100) plane of Zn2Co3(OH)10·2H2O) and 0.32 nm ((111) plane of CeO2), respectively. The CDs doped CeO2-MOH hetero-structure with the intimate interface can be formed in the system.

The chemical states are detected through the XPS analyses in Fig. 2 . Fig. 2a is the XPS spectrum of Ce 3d, four pairs of spin-orbit multiplets can be assigned in the curves, which labeled as µ (µ, µ1, µ2 and µ3) and ν (ν, ν1, ν2, ν3) are corresponded to the Ce 3d3/2 and Ce 3d5/2 states, respectively. The peaks at about 884 eV (ν1) and 902 eV (µ1) are indicated as Ce3+, and the other three, ν and µ, ν2 and µ2, ν3 and µ3, are the Ce4+ spin-orbit multiplets [24,25]. The Ce4+ ions are conversed into Ce3+ after carbon-doped process, and more Ce3+/Ce4+redox cycles are formed. There are almost no differencesof the band energies for the Co 2p between the two samples in the Fig. 2b. For the Zn 2p spectra in Fig. 2c, the peaks of Zn2 and Zn2ʹ are assigned to the Zn in ZnO wurtzite lattice. The other two species of Zn1 and Zn1ʹ are ascribed to ZnO-OH bond in hydroxide [26]. In Fig. 2d, the peak at around 529.6 eV is lattice oxygen, the peak at 531.6 eV indicates the surface oxygen vacancies (OVs), and the third peak at about 532.8 is the hydroxide or carbonyl groups in the composites [20]. Much more OVs can be formed on the exposed surface via the CDs doped process.

The photocatalytic N2 fixation rates under full spectrum (280 ∼ 780 nm) and visible light (420 ∼ 780 nm) irradiation are illustrated in Fig. 3 a. The CeO2-MOH sample has the lower activities, and the carbon dots doped catalyst has a good activity with about 528 and 256 µmol·gcal −1·h−1 under full spectrum and visible light irradiation for the CeO2-CH/CDs. The CDs-doped sample exhibits the excellent N2 photofixation rate, which was also competitive with the recent reports (Table S1). This sample also shows a relatively good catalytic reaction cycle performance. The introduction of carbondots as the conductor in the catalyst can improve the photocatalytic N2 fixation properties effectively.

From the diffuse reflectance UV–vis spectra in Fig. 4 a, the CeO2-MOH/CDs sample displays a higher absorption in the visible light range. The bandgaps with about 3.09 eV and 2.54 eV are confirmed for the CeO2 and MOH, respectively. Otherwise, the flat-band potential (Vfb) of CeO2 and MOH with about 0.14 V and −0.43 V (vs. NHE) are obtained from the Mott-Schottky plots curves in Fig. 4b, which are closed to the valence band potential (Vvb) bottom for the n-type semiconductors [27]. When MOH is attached on the CeO2 with a certain way, the hetero-structures will be formed at the interface. Apparently, the CeO2-MOH/CDs sample also has a lower PL intensity in Fig. 4c, and the separation efficiency of the photo induced electron-hole pair is highly improved for the CDs doped catalyst. The improved photocatalytic activity can be explained by Z-scheme heterojunction in Fig. 4d, of which CDs are as the charge transporting mediator [28]. The photo-inducedelectrons on the CB of CeO2 and holes on the VB of MOH can transfer quickly to the CDs surface. The holes and electrons are recombined on the VB of CeO2 and CB of CH, respectively. Moreover, the stronger reduction ability on CB of CeO2 (−0.43 V) and oxidation ability on VB of CH (3.23 V) can provide enough energy to accomplish the N2 fixaiton process.

In summary, CeO2-MOH/CDs heterojunction nanosheets with enhanced nitrogen fixation performances were constructed through a facile and economical fabrication strategy. The NH3 productivity can achieve about 528 and 256 µmol·gcal −1·h−1 without any sacrificial agents under the full spectrum and visible light illumination, respectively. The improved properties are due to the higher visible-light absorption and electron-hole separation efficiency for the CDs bridging Z-scheme heterojunction. This research affords a promising strategy to develop noble metal-free Z-scheme photocatalysts.

Huaiwei Zhang: Conceptualization, Writing – original draft. Shiting Wu: Methodology, Investigation. Liang Bao: Methodology, Investigation. Yifan Chen: Software. Jing-yuan Ge: Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The research funded by the Zhejiang Provincial Natural Science Foundation (LY20B010003 and LY20E020008).
